votes.reason: The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, implementing the exact same character mapping and string length/character-by-character checks.

The Python code correctly implements the character shifting logic and string validation exactly as specified in the Coq code.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The char_relation function maps lowercase letters a-z to their +4 shifted counterparts (with wraparound), and leaves other characters unchanged, matching the Coq definition. The problem_89_pre always returns True, and problem_89_spec checks that lengths match and each character pair satisfies char_relation, exactly as specified in Coq.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The char_relation function maps lowercase letters a-z to their +4 rotated equivalents (with wrap-around), and leaves other characters unchanged, matching the Coq definition. The problem_89_spec function checks that lengths are equal and that each character pair satisfies char_relation, which is exactly what the Coq spec requires. The precondition is trivially True in both cases.

Python spec checks equal length and per-character mapping of a..z shifted by 4 with wraparound, leaving all other chars unchanged, matching the Coq char_relation and overall spec.
Python maps lowercase letters by +4 with wraparound and leaves other chars unchanged, and checks length and per-index relation exactly as the Coq spec does.
